attributes.stem: A 52-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for an eye examination. She has no visual symptoms. Her mother was recently diagnosed with glaucoma and advised her to present to her GP for a review. She had laser-assisted in situ keratomileusis successfully eight years prior, and takes no regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her visual acuity is 6/6 in both eyes with correction. Intraocular pressures are 25 mmHg in the right eye and 26 mmHg in the left eye on two separate occasions (normal range 10- 21 mmHg).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
options: Magnetic resonance imaging of the orbits
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with elevated intraocular pressure (ocular hypertension) and a family history of glaucoma, putting her at risk of developing glaucoma. The key distinction between ocular hypertension and glaucoma is the presence of optic nerve damage and visual field defects in glaucoma. Her risk is increased by her family history of glaucoma, being over the age of 40 and having previous eye surgery. Myopia and thin corneas are also risk factors for intraocular pressure. Careful monitoring must occur to further investigate the high intraocular pressure and screen for early glaucoma.

Optical coherence tomography (OCT) is the most appropriate next investigation as it provides detailed imaging of the optic nerve head and retinal nerve fibre layer thickness. This baseline assessment is crucial for monitoring any progression to glaucoma over time. OCT can detect early structural changes before functional visual field defects develop leading to glaucoma.

Fluorescein angiography is used to assess retinal vasculature and is not indicated in the evaluation of ocular hypertension or glaucoma. It is primarily used for conditions such as diabetic retinopathy or age-related macular degeneration.

Magnetic resonance imaging of the orbits would be indicated if there was concern for orbital masses or neurological causes of optic nerve damage. There are no features in this presentation to suggest the need for neuroimaging.

B-scan ultrasonography is useful for evaluating intraocular structures when direct visualization is impaired, such as in cases of dense cataracts or vitreous hemorrhage. It is not routinely used in the assessment of ocular hypertension.

Conjunctival swab is used to identify infectious organisms in cases of conjunctivitis or other ocular surface infections. It has no role in the assessment of ocular hypertension.

After OCT imaging, this patient would also require visual field testing, gonioscopy to assess the anterior chamber angle, and central corneal thickness measurement, as these are all important components in the complete assessment of ocular hypertension and glaucoma risk.
learningPoints: Goldmann applanation tonometry is the gold standard for measuring intraocular pressure.
Central corneal thickness measurement is essential in the assessment of ocular hypertension as it affects tonometry readings.
Visual field testing and optic disc assessment are crucial components in differentiating ocular hypertension from glaucoma.
references: Boey P, Mansberger S.  Ocular hypertension: an approach to assessment and management.  Canadian Journal of Opthalmology. 2014: 49 (6).  Available at: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0008418214002373

Kaushik S, Pandav S. An Overview of Current Understanding in Ocular Hypertension.  Journal of Current Glaucoma Practice. 2011.  Available at: https://www.researchgate.net/publication/287034422_An_Overview_of_Current_Understanding_in_Ocular_Hypertension
